source.title: Fatal Release of Acetic Acid and Methyl Iodide Mixture at LyondellBasell La Porte Complex

actions_taken: LyondellBasell transported 29 personnel to medical facilities for further evaluation and treatment
LyondellBasell installed tamper-resistant mechanisms and tags on the valve cover fasteners of actuated plug valves within the unit after the incident
event.summary: On July 27, 2021, three Turn2 Specialty Companies contract workers at the LyondellBasell La Porte Complex were removing a pneumatic actuator from a pressurised eight-inch plug valve in the acetic acid unit. The workers inadvertently removed pressure-retaining valve cover nuts instead of only the actuator mounting bolts, then used a pry bar on the coupler, causing the valve cover and plug to eject. Approximately 164,000 pounds of acetic acid mixture at 238°F and 130 psi erupted from the open valve. Two workers were fatally injured from chemical burns and toxic inhalation of acetic acid and methyl iodide; a third worker was seriously injured. A LyondellBasell responder was also seriously injured, and 29 additional personnel were transported to medical facilities. Property damage including loss of use was estimated at $40 million. Contributing factors included a plug valve design lacking sufficient features to prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components, the absence of written procedures for actuator removal, and the absence of worker training on the task.
recommendations: 2021-05-I-TX-R1: LyondellBasell to update policy documents to require procedures for removing actuating equipment from plug valves, identify pressure-retaining vs non-pressure-retaining components, ensure training, control hazardous energy per 29 CFR 1910.147, and require risk assessments before work is authorised.

2021-05-I-TX-R2: LyondellBasell to require competent employee verification that contractors are competent, trained, and qualified; provide oversight of contractor work on process equipment; inform contractors of process hazards and provide equipment-specific procedures.
2021-05-I-TX-R3: Turn2 to update policy documents to require written, detailed procedures for work on process equipment and training before work is authorised.

2021-05-I-TX-R4: ASME to revise Standard B16.34 to require marking of pressure-retaining components on existing plug valves and require new plug valves be designed per Prevention through Design principles to prevent inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components.

2021-05-I-TX-R5: API to revise Standard 599 to acknowledge past incidents, recommend written procedures for actuator/gearbox removal, require marking of pressure-retaining components, and require new plug valve designs per Prevention through Design principles.

2021-05-I-TX-R6: VMA Technical Committee to work with ASME and API to develop a white paper recommending written procedures, marking of pressure-retaining components, and Prevention through Design for new plug valve designs.

hazards.name: Pressurised acetic acid and methyl iodide mixture in acetic acid reactor and connected piping (130 psi, 238°F, ~164,000 lb)

threats.name: Inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining plug valve components during actuator removal maintenance task
Inadequate plug valve design — insufficient design features to distinguish and prevent removal of pressure-retaining components
Absence of written procedures for plug valve actuator removal
Lack of worker training on actuator removal steps and pressure-retaining component identification
Inadequate contractor oversight and competency verification by operating company

consequences.name: Fatal injuries to workers — two Turn2 workers fatally injured by chemical burns and toxic inhalation of acetic acid and methyl iodide
Serious injuries — third Turn2 worker and a LyondellBasell responder seriously injured; 29 additional personnel transported to medical facilities
Major property damage and loss of use — estimated $40 million
Large-scale toxic chemical release — approximately 164,000 pounds of acetic acid mixture including methyl iodide released to atmosphere

controls.name: Plug valve design with actuator-removable-without-pressure-retention-impact (API 599 compliant design)
controls.barrier_role: Engineered valve design intended to allow actuator removal without disturbing pressure-retaining components
controls.lod_basis: Primary engineered safeguard — valve design intended to prevent inadvertent pressure boundary breach during actuator removal

supporting_text: The CSB notes that the design of the plug valve involved in the LyondellBasell incident was of a newer design recommended by the American Petroleum Institute (API) Standard 599 Metal Plug Valves—Flanged, Threaded, and Welding Ends that would allow an actuator to be mounted or removed without affecting the pressure retention of the valve.
This newer design, however, did not prevent workers from inadvertently removing pressure retaining components while removing the actuator.
Contributing to the incident was a plug valve design that did not include sufficient design features to prevent the inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining valve components.

controls.name: Marking / labelling of pressure-retaining components on plug valves (e.g. paint, warning signs)
controls.barrier_role: Visual differentiation of pressure-retaining from non-pressure-retaining fasteners to prevent inadvertent removal
controls.lod_basis: Passive engineered/administrative visual barrier to prevent human error in component identification

supporting_text: One of the workers at the LyondellBasell facility mentioned to the CSB, 'Nothing was marked. None of the bolts were labeled 'remove' [or] 'don't remove.'

The CSB concludes that had the LyondellBasell plug valve pressure-retaining bolts been clearly identified (such as through paint markings or a warning label or sign), the work crew might have removed only the bolts securing the actuator to the plug valve, which would have prevented the incident.

After the incident, LyondellBasell installed tamper-resistant mechanisms and tags on the valve cover fasteners of actuated plug valves within the unit to help prevent the inadvertent removal of pressure-retaining components while the valves are in service.

controls.name: Written procedure for plug valve actuator removal
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control specifying correct steps, identifying pressure-retaining vs non-pressure-retaining components, and precautions to prevent inadvertent valve disassembly
controls.lod_basis: Administrative barrier — written procedure as a layer of defence against human error during maintenance task

supporting_text: LyondellBasell did not have a procedure detailing how to remove the actuator, and neither LyondellBasell nor Turn2 trained the Turn2 personnel on how to remove the actuator.
LyondellBasell did not provide the work crew with a procedure or instructions on how to remove the actuator from the plug valve.
When asked if they had a written procedure for the task, one Turn2 worker stated, '…not at all. I think that would have been pretty helpful.'

Had LyondellBasell provided a procedure to the Turn2 work crew that detailed the bolts to be removed for the actuator removal work and warned against removing the pressure-retaining bolts, the incident could have been prevented.

controls.name: Worker training on actuator removal procedure and pressure-retaining component identification
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control ensuring workers understand which components are pressure-retaining and the correct sequence for actuator removal
controls.lod_basis: Administrative barrier — competency-based training as a layer of defence against human error

supporting_text: Neither LyondellBasell nor Turn2 trained the Turn2 work crew on the steps necessary to remove the actuator.
Had the Turn2 work crew been trained on which bolts to remove to safely remove the actuator from the plug valve, the incident might not have occurred.
The lack of training for the workers conducting the work [contributed to the incident].
The CSB did not find documentation indicating the work crew had experience installing, repairing, or removing valves or valve actuators.

controls.name: Pre-job risk assessment for plug valve actuator removal work
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to evaluate hazards, determine if de-inventorying or depressurisation is required, and ensure adequate safeguards before work is authorised
controls.lod_basis: Administrative barrier — formal risk assessment as a management layer of defence before hazardous maintenance work

supporting_text: LyondellBasell personnel did not consider the option of de-inventorying the reactor and connected piping (or otherwise isolating the valve from hazardous energy) before the actuator removal work could be authorized, as the planned actuator removal should not have involved opening the process equipment.
Both LyondellBasell and Turn2 personnel perceived the actuator removal to be a simple task with minimal risks.
LyondellBasell did not adequately assess the potential risk of exposing the contract crew to hazardous chemicals during the actuator removal in light of historical incidents in the industry.

controls.name: Contractor competency verification and oversight by LyondellBasell competent employee
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to verify contractor qualifications and provide on-site oversight during work on process equipment containing hazardous materials
controls.lod_basis: Administrative barrier — management oversight as a layer of defence against unqualified work execution

supporting_text: LyondellBasell did not sufficiently determine that the contractors performing the work were competent, adequately trained, or qualified to perform the actuator removal, and LyondellBasell did not provide sufficient oversight of the actuator removal task.
The LyondellBasell operator then left the area, and the Turn2 foreman and two Turn2 pipefitters began work to remove the plug valve actuator.

When contractors are conducting work on or near plant equipment containing hazardous materials, it is critical for the operating company to oversee the contractor work and ensure that it is conducted in a safe manner.
The CSB did not find documentation indicating the work crew had experience installing, repairing, or removing valves or valve actuators.

controls.name: Work permit system
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control authorising the maintenance task and providing a formal checkpoint before work commences
controls.lod_basis: Administrative barrier — permit-to-work as a management layer of defence

supporting_text: At around 6:45 p.m., LyondellBasell issued the work permit for the task.
LyondellBasell did not have a procedure detailing how to remove the actuator, and neither LyondellBasell nor Turn2 trained the Turn2 personnel on how to remove the actuator.
Both LyondellBasell and Turn2 personnel perceived the actuator removal to be a simple task with minimal risks.

controls.name: Energy isolation / lockout-tagout (LOTO) procedure
controls.barrier_role: Administrative and engineering control to isolate hazardous energy (pressure, chemical) before maintenance work on process equipment
controls.lod_basis: Primary administrative/engineering barrier — energy isolation to prevent exposure to pressurised process fluid during maintenance

supporting_text: LyondellBasell's Energy Isolation Procedure specified that the only pneumatically actuated control valves that were deemed to be approved energy isolation devices were those equipped with manual hand jacks.

LyondellBasell personnel decided that they would remove the actuator connected to the plug valve (including its coupler) so that a pipe tee could be installed over the valve stem… thus meeting the requirements for having a physical lock installed.

LyondellBasell personnel did not consider the option of de-inventorying the reactor and connected piping (or otherwise isolating the valve from hazardous energy) before the actuator removal work could be authorized.
Ensure that hazardous energy is controlled when performing these procedures, as required by 29 C.F.R. 1910.147.

controls.name: Emergency response and medical treatment
controls.barrier_role: Emergency response to minimise harm to injured workers and other personnel following the chemical release
controls.lod_basis: Recovery barrier — emergency response and medical treatment after the top event

supporting_text: LyondellBasell transported 29 other personnel to medical facilities for further evaluation and treatment.
A LyondellBasell responder were seriously injured.

The Turn2 foreman and one pipefitter were fatally injured from chemical burns and inhalation of the released acetic acid and methyl iodide, and the second pipefitter was seriously injured from acid exposure.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.

pif_ids_referenced.description: Situational awareness — workers did not recognise pressure-retaining nuts as distinct from actuator mounting bolts; no visual markings to aid identification
Competence — Turn2 work crew lacked documented experience with valve/actuator removal; no training provided on the specific task
Procedures — no written procedure existed for actuator removal from plug valve; task perceived as simple by both LyondellBasell and Turn2
Training — neither LyondellBasell nor Turn2 trained the work crew on actuator removal steps or pressure-retaining component identification
Supervision — LyondellBasell operator left the area after showing the crew the valve; no competent oversight during the work
Communication — LyondellBasell did not inform contractors of relevant process hazards or provide equipment-specific procedures
